content:
# Exodus Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~40v–41r (PDF pp. 76–77); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 54–55 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_014_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Trap Set by God (vv. 1–4)

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**2** "Tell the Israelites to turn back and camp in front of Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, facing Baal-zephon. Make camp opposite it, by the sea.

**3** Pharaoh will think, 'The Israelites are wandering around in confusion — the wilderness has closed in on them.'

**4** I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will chase after them. Then I will display my glory through Pharaoh and his entire army, and Egypt will know that I am YHWH." And they did so.

---

### Pharaoh Gives Chase (vv. 5–9)

**5** When the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his officials changed their minds about the people and said, "What have we done? We let Israel go — we have lost our slave labor force!"

**6** So he had his chariot harnessed and took his army with him —

**7** six hundred elite chariots and every other chariot in Egypt, each one crewed with officers.

**8** YHWH hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued the Israelites as they were marching out boldly.

**9** The Egyptians pursued them — all of Pharaoh's chariot horses, his riders, and his army — and overtook them where they were camped by the sea, near Pi-hahiroth, facing Baal-zephon.

---

### Israel's Terror and Moses' Command (vv. 10–14)

**10** As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up and saw the Egyptians bearing down on them. They were terrified and cried out to YHWH.

**11** They said to Moses, "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us out here to die in the wilderness? What have you done to us, dragging us out of Egypt?

**12** Isn't this exactly what we told you in Egypt: 'Leave us alone and let us serve the Egyptians'? It would have been better to serve Egypt than to die out here in the wilderness!"

**13** Moses said to the people, "Do not be afraid. Stand firm and watch — you are about to see the deliverance that YHWH will accomplish for you today. The Egyptians you see before you today, you will never see again.

**14** YHWH will fight for you. You need only be still."

---

### YHWH's Command to Move (vv. 15–18)

**15** YHWH said to Moses, "Why are you crying out to me? Tell the Israelites to move forward.

**16** Raise your staff and stretch your hand out over the sea and divide it, so the Israelites can go through the sea on dry ground.

**17** As for me — I am about to harden the hearts of the Egyptians so that they will follow the Israelites in. Then I will display my glory through Pharaoh and his entire army, through his chariots and his horsemen.

**18** And Egypt will know that I am YHWH, when I have displayed my glory through Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen."

---

### The Angel and the Cloud Shift Position (vv. 19–20)

**19** The angel of God, who had been traveling in front of Israel's camp, moved and went behind them. The pillar of cloud shifted from in front of them and took its stand behind them,

**20** coming between the Egyptian camp and the Israelite camp. The cloud brought darkness to one side and light to the other, so that neither camp came near the other all through the night.

---

### The Sea Divided (vv. 21–22)

**21** Then Moses stretched his hand out over the sea, and YHWH drove the sea back with a powerful east wind that blew all night long. He turned the sea into dry ground, and the waters were split apart.

**22** The Israelites went through the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on their right and on their left.

---

### YHWH Throws the Egyptian Army into Chaos (vv. 23–25)

**23** The Egyptians gave chase — all of Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen — following them right into the middle of the sea.

**24** During the last watch of the night, YHWH looked down on the Egyptian army through the pillar of fire and cloud, and he threw them into a panic.

**25** He jammed the wheels of their chariots so that they could barely move. The Egyptians said, "Let us get away from Israel! YHWH is fighting for them against Egypt!"

---

### The Sea Returns (vv. 26–29)

**26** YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch your hand out over the sea, so the water flows back over the Egyptians — over their chariots and their horsemen."

**27** Moses stretched his hand out over the sea, and at dawn the sea returned to its normal course. The Egyptians were fleeing straight into it, and YHWH swept them into the middle of the sea.

**28** The water flowed back and covered the chariots and the horsemen — Pharaoh's entire army that had followed them into the sea. Not one of them survived.

**29** But the Israelites had walked through the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on their right and on their left.

---

### Deliverance Complete — Faith Born from Sight (vv. 30–31)

**30** That day YHWH delivered Israel from the power of Egypt, and Israel saw the Egyptians lying dead on the seashore.

**31** When Israel saw the mighty power that YHWH had unleashed against Egypt, the people were filled with awe before YHWH. They put their trust in YHWH and in Moses his servant.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.2 — "TURN BACK AND CAMP" — THE DIVINE TRAP (Heb. *dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyashuvu veyachanu lifnei Pi haChirot bein Migdol uvein hayyam lifnei Ba'al Tsefon nikhocho tachanu al-hayyam*, דַּבֵּר֮ אֶל־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵל֒ וְיָשֻׁ֗בוּ וְיַחֲנוּ֙ לִפְנֵי֙ פִּ֣י הַחִירֹ֔ת בֵּ֥ין מִגְדֹּ֖ל וּבֵ֣ין הַיָּ֑ם לִפְנֵי֙ בַּ֣עַל צְפֹ֔ן נִכְח֥וֹ תַחֲנ֖וּ עַל־הַיָּֽם):** The command *veyashuvu* (וישבו, "let them turn back") is stunning: God instructs Israel to reverse direction. They have been marching away from Egypt; now God tells them to turn around and camp in what will look like a strategic blunder — hemmed in between the wilderness and the sea. The place names pile up with geographic precision: *Pi haChirot* (פי החירת, "mouth of the gorges/freedom" — the etymology is debated; some connect *chirot* to *cherut*, חרות, "freedom," making it ironically "the mouth of freedom"), *Migdol* (מגדול, "tower, fortress" — an Egyptian military outpost), and *Ba'al Tsefon* (בעל צפון, "Lord of the North" — a Canaanite deity whose shrine apparently stood on the coast). Israel camps between an Egyptian fort and a pagan shrine, facing the sea with the wilderness at their back. Every direction spells death.

This is not a mistake. The verb *veyashuvu* is in the jussive — a deliberate command, not a wrong turn. God is staging the scene. He is arranging the geography so that the deliverance will be unmistakably his. There is no human escape route. The Vaticanus reads *kai apostrepsantes stratopedeusatōsin* — "and having turned back, let them make camp" — preserving the deliberate reversal.

**v.3 — "PHARAOH WILL THINK" (Heb. *ve'amar Par'oh livnei Yisra'el nevukhim hem ba'arets sagar aleihem hammidbar*, וְאָמַ֤ר פַּרְעֹה֙ לִבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל נְבֻכִ֥ים הֵ֖ם בָּאָ֑רֶץ סָגַ֥ר עֲלֵיהֶ֖ם הַמִּדְבָּֽר):** The word *nevukhim* (נבכים, "confused, wandering aimlessly, entangled") is rare, appearing only here and in Joel 1:18 and Esther 3:15. Its root *navakh* (נבך) suggests bewilderment, disorientation — people who do not know where they are going. The verb *sagar* (סגר, "to shut, to close, to lock") portrays the wilderness as a prison that has "locked" Israel in. Pharaoh reads the situation as military incompetence: a fleeing slave population has gotten lost and boxed itself in. He is reading the scene exactly as God designed him to read it. The trap is not for Israel — it is for Pharaoh. God has arranged the chessboard so that Pharaoh will see an irresistible opportunity and rush into the killing ground.

**v.4 — FINAL DIVINE HARDENING (Heb. *vechizzaqti et-lev Par'oh veradaf achareihem ve'ikkavdah bePar'oh uvkhol-cheilo veyed'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH*, וְחִזַּקְתִּ֣י אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֗ה וְרָדַ֣ף אַחֲרֵיהֶם֮ וְאִכָּבְדָ֣ה בְפַרְעֹה֮ וּבְכָל־חֵילוֹ֒ וְיָדְע֤וּ מִצְרַ֨יִם֙ כִּי־אֲנִ֣י יְהוָ֔ה):** The verb *vechizzaqti* (וחזקתי, from *chazaq*, חזק, "to be strong, to harden") is in the Piel — the intensive active stem — with YHWH as the explicit subject: "I will strengthen/harden Pharaoh's heart." This is the final occurrence of the divine hardening in the narrative proper (before the retrospective summary of v.8). God is not merely permitting Pharaoh's stubbornness; he is intensifying it for a specific purpose.

That purpose is expressed by the verb *ve'ikkavdah* (ואכבדה, from *kavad*, כבד, "to be heavy, to be honored, to be glorious"). The Niphal form means "I will be glorified" or "I will display my weightiness." Critically, *kavad* is the same root as the hardening verb *hikbid* (הכביד, "to make heavy") used of Pharaoh's heart throughout the plague narrative. God's *kavod* (glory/weight) is displayed precisely through Pharaoh's *koved lev* (heaviness of heart). The stubbornness of the tyrant becomes the canvas for the glory of God. The heavier Pharaoh's heart, the heavier — more glorious — YHWH's deliverance.

The knowledge formula — *veyed'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH* ("and Egypt will know that I am YHWH") — states the theological purpose of the entire sea event: Egypt must *know*. Not just Israel, but Egypt. The exodus is not a private salvation; it is a public revelation. Pharaoh asked "Who is YHWH?" (5:2); the sea will answer.

**v.5 — "THE PEOPLE HAD FLED" (Heb. *vayyuggad lemelekh Mitsrayim ki varach ha'am vayyehafekh levav Par'oh va'avadav el-ha'am vayomru mah-zzot asinu ki-shillachnu et-Yisra'el me'ovdenu*, וַיֻּגַּד֙ לְמֶ֣לֶךְ מִצְרַ֔יִם כִּ֥י בָרַ֖ח הָעָ֑ם וַ֠יֵּהָפֵ֠ךְ לְבַ֨ב פַּרְעֹ֤ה וַעֲבָדָיו֙ אֶל־הָעָ֔ם וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ מַה־זֹּ֣את עָשִׂ֔ינוּ כִּֽי־שִׁלַּ֥חְנוּ אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל מֵעָבְדֵֽנוּ):** The verb *varach* (ברח, "fled") is significant — it is how Pharaoh perceives the departure. From Israel's perspective, they were *sent out* (*shillach*); from Pharaoh's perspective, they *fled*. The verb *vayyehafekh* (ויהפך, "was turned, was reversed") from *hafakh* (הפך, "to turn, to overturn") describes a complete reversal of heart. The same verb describes the overturning of Sodom (Gen. 19:25). Pharaoh's heart literally flips — the grief of the firstborn dead is replaced by the rage of a slaveholder who has lost his property.

The anguished question — *mah-zzot asinu* (מה זאת עשינו, "what is this we have done?") — is remarkable. After ten plagues, after burying their firstborn, after begging Israel to leave (12:33), Pharaoh and his officials now frame the exodus as their own mistake. The verb *me'ovdenu* (מעבדנו, "from serving us") strips away all pretense: Israel was not a guest population. They were a labor force. Pharaoh's grief is not humanitarian. It is economic.

**vv.7-8 — SIX HUNDRED ELITE CHARIOTS (Heb. *vayyiqqach shesh-me'ot rekhev bachur vekhol rekhev Mitsrayim veshalishim al-kullo*, וַיִּקַּ֗ח שֵׁשׁ־מֵא֥וֹת רֶ֨כֶב֙ בָּח֔וּר וְכֹ֖ל רֶ֣כֶב מִצְרָ֑יִם וְשָׁלִשִׁ֖ם עַל־כֻּלּֽוֹ):** The number *shesh-me'ot* (שש מאות, "six hundred") specifies the elite strike force — *bachur* (בחור, "chosen, selected, prime") chariots. The *shalishim* (שלשם) are officers or warriors — the word derives from *shalosh* (שלש, "three"), possibly indicating a three-man chariot crew (driver, shield-bearer, archer) or third-ranking officers. The Vaticanus reads *tristatai* (τριστάται), likewise from the root for "three."

Verse 8 reintroduces the hardening: *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitsrayim* (ויחזק יהוה את לב פרעה, "and YHWH hardened the heart of Pharaoh"). This is the Piel again — YHWH as active agent. The phrase *beyad ramah* (ביד רמה, "with a high/raised hand") describes how Israel marched out — literally "with an exalted hand," a gesture of boldness, defiance, even triumph. The freed slaves do not slink away; they march with their hands raised. The Vaticanus renders this *cheiri hypsēlē* (χειρὶ ὑψηλῇ, "with a high hand").

**vv.10-12 — ISRAEL'S FIRST COMPLAINT: "WERE THERE NO GRAVES IN EGYPT?" (Heb. *hamin-beli ein-qevarim beMitsrayim leqachtanu lamut bammidbar mah-zzot asita lanu lehotsi'anu miMitsrayim*, הַמִבְּלִ֤י אֵין־קְבָרִים֙ בְּמִצְרַ֔יִם לְקַחְתָּ֖נוּ לָמ֣וּת בַּמִּדְבָּ֑ר מַה־זֹּאת֙ עָשִׂ֣יתָ לָּ֔נוּ לְהוֹצִיאָ֖נוּ מִמִּצְרָֽיִם):** This is one of the most brilliantly bitter lines in all of Scripture. The sarcasm is devastating: *hamin-beli ein-qevarim beMitsrayim* — "Was it because there are no graves in Egypt?" Egypt, the land of tombs and pyramids, the civilization that turned death into its defining industry — are its graves insufficient? Must we come out here to find new ones? The irony is multidimensional: Egypt's obsession with death is well known to the speakers, and they weaponize it. The triple negative construction (*hamin-beli ein*, "is it from the lack of there being none?") piles negation upon negation until the sentence almost collapses under its own sarcastic weight.

The complaint reveals the psychology of enslaved people confronting the cost of freedom. The phrase *tov lanu avod et-Mitsrayim mimmutenanu bammidbar* (טוב לנו עבד את מצרים ממתנו במדבר, "it is better for us to serve Egypt than to die in the wilderness") — using the infinitive *avod* (עבד, "to serve/be enslaved") — is the first articulation of what will become a recurring pattern: the nostalgia of the oppressed for the predictability of their oppression. Slavery is known; freedom is dangerous. A living slave is, in purely biological terms, better off than a free corpse. This calculus will haunt the entire wilderness narrative.

**vv.13-14 — "STAND FIRM AND SEE" — MOSES' GREATEST LINE (Heb. *al-tira'u hityatsvu ur'u et-yeshu'at YHWH asher-ya'aseh lakhem hayyom ki asher re'item et-Mitsrayim hayyom lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam; YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun*, אַל־תִּירָ֗אוּ הִֽתְיַצְב֗וּ וּרְאוּ֙ אֶת־יְשׁוּעַ֣ת יְהוָ֔ה אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂ֥ה לָכֶ֖ם הַיּ֑וֹם כִּ֗י אֲשֶׁ֨ר רְאִיתֶ֤ם אֶת־מִצְרַ֨יִם֙ הַיּ֔וֹם לֹ֥א תֹסִ֛פוּ לִרְאֹתָ֥ם ע֖וֹד עַד־עוֹלָֽם יְהוָ֖ה יִלָּחֵ֣ם לָכֶ֑ם וְאַתֶּ֖ם תַּחֲרִשֽׁוּן):** Three imperatives frame Moses' response to the people's panic. *Al-tira'u* (אל תיראו, "do not fear!") — the most common divine command in Scripture. *Hityatsvu* (התיצבו, from *yatsav*, יצב, "to station oneself, to take one's stand") — the Hithpael reflexive, meaning "station yourselves, plant yourselves" — the verb of a soldier holding a position, refusing to retreat. And *ur'u* (וראו, "and see!") — the imperative of sight. Do not fear. Stand. Watch. Three actions, one sequence: conquer terror, hold ground, open your eyes.

The noun *yeshu'ah* (ישועה, "salvation, deliverance, rescue") — from *yasha'* (ישע, "to save, to deliver") — is the word that gives us the name *Yeshua/Yehoshua* ("Joshua/Jesus"). This is not abstract spiritual salvation; it is concrete physical rescue from an army about to slaughter them. The *yeshu'ah* of YHWH is, at this moment, a military deliverance.

The promise — *lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam* (לא תספו לראתם עוד עד עולם, "you will never see them again, ever") — is absolute. The phrase *ad-olam* (עד עולם, "until forever/eternity") transforms a military prediction into a cosmic declaration. This is not merely "you won't see these soldiers again." It is "the Egypt that enslaved you is finished as a reality in your lives."

Verse 14 contains what may be the most compressed theology of divine warfare in the Bible: *YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun* — "YHWH will fight for you, and you — be silent." The verb *tacharishun* (תחרשון, from *charash*, חרש, "to be silent, to be still, to plow") is striking. It does not mean "do nothing" — it means "stop talking." Their complaints, their sarcasm, their death-wish nostalgia for Egypt — all of it: be silent. The silence is not passivity; it is trust made audible by the absence of protest.

**v.15 — "WHY ARE YOU CRYING OUT TO ME?" (Heb. *mah-tits'aq elai dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyissa'u*, מַה־תִּצְעַ֖ק אֵלָ֑י דַּבֵּ֥ר אֶל־בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל וְיִסָּֽעוּ):** YHWH's rebuke to Moses is unexpected. Moses had just told the people to stand still and watch; now YHWH says, "Why are you crying out to me? Tell them to *move*." The verb *veyissa'u* (ויסעו, from *nasa'*, נסע, "to pull up stakes, to set out, to journey") is the standard verb for breaking camp and marching. The tension between Moses' "stand still" (v.13) and YHWH's "move forward" (v.15) is deliberate: there is a time for watching and a time for walking. Moses discerned correctly that YHWH would act; but the people are not spectators at a performance — they must walk through the deliverance. God parts the sea, but Israel must enter it.

**v.16 — "DIVIDE IT" (Heb. *ve'attah harem et-mattekha uneteh et-yadkha al-hayyam uveqa'ehu*, וְאַתָּ֞ה הָרֵ֣ם אֶת־מַטְּךָ֗ וּנְטֵ֧ה אֶת־יָדְךָ֛ עַל־הַיָּ֖ם וּבְקָעֵ֑הוּ):** The verb *uveqa'ehu* (ובקעהו, from *baqa'*, בקע, "to split, to cleave, to rend") is the same verb used for splitting wood (Eccl. 10:9), splitting rocks for water (Ps. 78:15), and the splitting of the earth (Num. 16:31). It is a verb of violent rupture — not gentle parting but forceful division. The sea does not politely recede; it is torn open. The same root appears in the prophetic vision of Zechariah 14:4, where the Mount of Olives will be *split* in two. God's deliverances are not subtle.

**vv.19-20 — THE ANGEL AND CLOUD SHIFT TO THE REAR (Heb. *vayyissa mal'akh ha'Elohim haholekh lifnei machaneh Yisra'el vayyeelekh me'achareihem vayyissa ammud he'anan mippneihem vayyammod me'achareihem; vayyavo bein machaneh Mitsrayim uvein machaneh Yisra'el vayehi he'anan vehachoshekh vayyaer et-hallailah velo-qarav zeh el-zeh kol-hallailah*, וַיִּסַּ֞ע מַלְאַ֣ךְ הָאֱלֹהִ֗ים הַהֹלֵךְ֙ לִפְנֵי֙ מַחֲנֵ֣ה יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וַיֵּ֖לֶךְ מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶ֑ם וַיִּסַּ֞ע עַמּ֤וּד הֶֽעָנָן֙ מִפְּנֵיהֶ֔ם וַיַּֽעֲמֹ֖ד מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶֽם וַיָּבֹ֞א בֵּ֣ין ׀ מַחֲנֵ֣ה מִצְרַ֗יִם וּבֵין֙ מַחֲנֵ֣ה יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וַיְהִ֤י הֶעָנָן֙ וְהַחֹ֔שֶׁךְ וַיָּ֖אֶר אֶת־הַלָּ֑יְלָה וְלֹא־קָרַ֥ב זֶ֛ה אֶל־זֶ֖ה כָּל־הַלָּֽיְלָה):** The *mal'akh ha'Elohim* (מלאך האלהים, "the angel/messenger of God") who has been leading the march (*haholekh lifnei*, "the one going before") now relocates to the rear (*me'achareihem*, "behind them"). The pillar of cloud follows suit. The theological shift is profound: the God who leads from the front now shields from the rear. The guide becomes the guard. The same divine presence that draws Israel forward now stands between Israel and its pursuing enemy.

Verse 20 is textually difficult. The Hebrew reads *vayehi he'anan vehachoshekh vayyaer et-hallailah* — literally "and there was the cloud and the darkness, and it illuminated the night." The cloud that brings darkness to the Egyptians simultaneously illuminates the night for Israel. The same object produces opposite effects depending on which side you stand on. This is a masterful literary image of how divine presence is simultaneously judgment and salvation, depending on one's relationship to the God behind the cloud. The Vaticanus reads *kai egeneto skotos kai gnophos kai diēlthen hē nyx* — "and there was darkness and gloom and the night passed" — a somewhat flattened reading that loses the paradox of simultaneous darkness and light.

**v.21 — THE EAST WIND ALL NIGHT (Heb. *vayyeit Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyolekh YHWH et-hayyam beruach qadim azzah kol-hallailah vayyasem et-hayyam lecharavah vayyibaq'u hammayim*, וַיֵּ֨ט מֹשֶׁ֣ה אֶת־יָדוֹ֮ עַל־הַיָּם֒ וַיּ֣וֹלֶךְ יְהוָ֣ה ׀ אֶת־הַ֠יָּ֠ם בְּר֨וּחַ קָדִ֤ים עַזָּה֙ כָּל־הַלַּ֔יְלָה וַיָּ֥שֶׂם אֶת־הַיָּ֖ם לֶחָרָבָ֑ה וַיִּבָּקְע֖וּ הַמָּֽיִם):** This verse combines human action and divine power with remarkable precision. Moses stretches his hand (*vayyeit*, from *natah*, נטה, "to stretch, to extend, to incline") — the same gesture commanded in v.16. But it is YHWH who acts upon the sea. The verb *vayyolekh* (ויולך, from *halakh*, הלך, in the Hiphil — "caused to go") means YHWH "drove" or "made the sea go" — the sea was commanded to move, and it obeyed.

The instrument is *ruach qadim azzah* (רוח קדים עזה, "a strong east wind"). The word *ruach* (רוח) means simultaneously "wind," "breath," and "spirit" — the same word used in Genesis 1:2, where the *ruach Elohim* hovers over the waters. The east wind (*qadim*, קדים) is the hot, fierce desert wind — the *sirocco* — coming from the Arabian wilderness. It is the destructive wind (Ezek. 17:10, 27:26; Jonah 4:8). The modifier *azzah* (עזה, "strong, fierce") intensifies it. And this wind blows *kol-hallailah* (כל הלילה, "all night long"). The miracle is not instantaneous. It takes hours. All through the darkness, while Israel waits and Egypt is held at bay by the cloud, the wind screams across the water.

The result is *lecharavah* (לחרבה, "into dry ground") — from *charev* (חרב, "to be dry, to be desolate"), the same root as *chorev* (Horeb, the mountain of God) and *cherev* (sword). Dry ground, desolation, and weaponry share a root — the sea floor becomes a desert, and the desert becomes a road.

**v.22 — WALLS OF WATER (Heb. *vayyavo'u venei-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabbashah vehamayim lahem chomah miiminam umiss'molam*, וַיָּבֹ֧אוּ בְנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל בְּת֥וֹךְ הַיָּ֖ם בַּיַּבָּשָׁ֑ה וְהַמַּ֤יִם לָהֶם֙ חוֹמָ֔ה מִֽימִינָ֖ם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָֽם):** The word *chomah* (חומה, "wall") — the same word used for a city wall (Josh. 2:15, the wall of Jericho) — portrays the water as architecture. The sea has been restructured into a building. Water, which is formless, has become structural, load-bearing, vertical. The phrase *miiminam umiss'molam* (מימינם ומשמאלם, "on their right and on their left") places Israel in a corridor — a hallway of water, a passage between liquid walls. The word *bayyabbashah* (ביבשה, "on dry ground") appears for the second time (after v.16's command), now fulfilled. What was commanded is accomplished. What was promised is underfoot.

The image is both terrifying and protecting. To walk between walls of water is to walk inside a miracle that could collapse at any moment. The dry ground underfoot is a gift; the walls on either side are both shield and threat. Israel walks through the sea trusting that the walls will hold — that the same God who split the water will keep it split long enough. This is what faith looks like as a physical experience: walking forward on ground that should not exist, between walls that should not stand.

**v.24 — "YHWH LOOKED DOWN THROUGH THE PILLAR" (Heb. *vayehi be'ashmoret habboqer vayyashqef YHWH el-machaneh Mitsrayim be'ammud esh ve'anan vayyahom et machaneh Mitsrayim*, וַיְהִ֗י בְּאַשְׁמֹ֨רֶת֙ הַבֹּ֔קֶר וַיַּשְׁקֵ֤ף יְהוָה֙ אֶל־מַחֲנֵ֣ה מִצְרַ֔יִם בְּעַמּ֥וּד אֵ֖שׁ וְעָנָ֑ן וַיָּ֕הָם אֵ֖ת מַחֲנֵ֥ה מִצְרָֽיִם):** The *ashmoret habboqer* (אשמרת הבקר, "the morning watch") is the last of the three night watches in the ancient Israelite system — approximately 2:00 to 6:00 AM, the darkest hour before dawn. The verb *vayyashqef* (וישקף, from *shaqaf*, שקף, "to look down, to gaze down, to peer") is the same verb used when YHWH "looked down" on Sodom before its destruction (Gen. 19:28) and when YHWH will "look down" from heaven (Deut. 26:15). When YHWH *shaqaf*, judgment follows. The verb has an almost physical quality — like a face peering out of a high window.

The image is extraordinary: YHWH looks through (*be-*, through the medium of) the pillar of fire and cloud — as though the pillar is a window, or a weapon-sight. He gazes down at the Egyptian army caught in the middle of the sea, and the gaze itself brings *mehumah* (מהומה): the verb *vayyahom* (ויהם, from *hamam*, המם, "to throw into confusion, to panic, to rout") is the standard verb for divinely induced military panic (Josh. 10:10; Judg. 4:15; 1 Sam. 7:10). The confusion is not psychological only — it is supernatural. YHWH's gaze breaks the army's cohesion.

**v.25 — JAMMED WHEELS (Heb. *vayyasar et offan markevotav vayenahagehu bikhvedut vayyomer Mitsrayim anusah mippnei Yisra'el ki YHWH nilcham lahem beMitsrayim*, וַיָּ֗סַר אֵ֚ת אֹפַ֣ן מַרְכְּבֹתָ֔יו וַֽיְנַהֲגֵ֖הוּ בִּכְבֵדֻ֑ת וַיֹּ֣אמֶר מִצְרַ֗יִם אָנ֨וּסָה֙ מִפְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל כִּ֣י יְהוָ֔ה נִלְחָ֥ם לָהֶ֖ם בְּמִצְרָֽיִם):** The verb *vayyasar* (ויסר, from *sur*, סור, "to remove, to turn aside") — YHWH "removed" or "jammed" the chariot wheels. The word *offan* (אפן, "wheel") is singular, used collectively. The phrase *bikhvedut* (בכבדת, "with heaviness, with difficulty") contains the root *kavad* (כבד) — the same root as *kavod* ("glory") and the hardening of Pharaoh's heart. The chariots move "heavily." The heaviness that was in Pharaoh's heart is now in his chariot wheels. The metaphor has become physical. What was internal stubbornness is now external immobility.

The Egyptian soldiers' cry — *anusah mippnei Yisra'el* (אנוסה מפני ישראל, "let us flee from Israel!") — reverses the entire power dynamic. Egypt, the superpower, flees from Israel, the slave nation. The reason they give — *ki YHWH nilcham lahem beMitsrayim* ("for YHWH fights for them against Egypt") — is the theological recognition that Moses promised in v.14. What Moses declared as prophecy, the Egyptians now confess as experience. The verb *nilcham* (נלחם, Niphal of *lacham*, "to fight") echoes Moses' exact word. Even the enemies testify.

**v.27 — THE SEA RETURNS AT DAWN (Heb. *vayyeit Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyashov hayyam lifnot boqer le'eitano uMitsrayim nasim liqrato vayyena'er YHWH et-Mitsrayim betokh hayyam*, וַיֵּ֨ט מֹשֶׁ֣ה אֶת־יָדוֹ֮ עַל־הַיָּם֒ וַיָּ֨שָׁב הַיָּ֜ם לִפְנ֥וֹת בֹּ֨קֶר֙ לְאֵ֣יתָנ֔וֹ וּמִצְרַ֖יִם נָסִ֣ים לִקְרָאת֑וֹ וַיְנַעֵ֧ר יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־מִצְרַ֖יִם בְּת֥וֹךְ הַיָּֽם):** The phrase *lifnot boqer* (לפנות בקר, "at the turning of the morning," i.e., at dawn) uses *panah* (פנה, "to turn, to face") — the morning "turns" toward them, dawn arrives. The word *le'eitano* (לאיתנו, "to its strength/normal flow/perennial state") from *eitan* (איתן, "strong, enduring, perennial") is striking: the sea returns to its *eitan* — its natural, permanent, powerful state. The splitting was temporary; the sea's power is permanent. The miracle was a parenthesis in the sea's eternal existence.

The verb *vayyena'er* (וינער, from *na'ar*, נער, "to shake off, to shake out") is astonishing: YHWH "shook off" or "shook out" the Egyptians into the sea — like one shakes insects from a garment, or dust from a cloth. The military superpower of the ancient world is flicked into the sea like debris. The Vaticanus reads *kai exetinaxen kyrios tous Aigyptious meson tēs thalassēs* — "and the Lord shook out the Egyptians into the middle of the sea" — preserving the same visceral verb.

**v.28 — "NOT ONE OF THEM SURVIVED" (Heb. *vayekhassu et-harekhev ve'et happarashim lekhol cheil Par'oh habba'im achareihem bayyam lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad*, וַיְכַסּ֣וּ אֶת־הָרֶ֗כֶב וְאֶת־הַפָּֽרָשִׁים֙ לְכֹל֙ חֵ֣יל פַּרְעֹ֔ה הַבָּאִ֥ים אַחֲרֵיהֶ֖ם בַּיָּ֑ם לֹֽא־נִשְׁאַ֥ר בָּהֶ֖ם עַד־אֶחָֽד):** The phrase *lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad* (לא נשאר בהם עד אחד, "there did not remain among them even one") is the language of total annihilation. The verb *nish'ar* (נשאר, from *sha'ar*, שאר, "to remain, to be left over") is negated absolutely: no remnant, no survivor, not even one. The military force that terrorized Israel — six hundred elite chariots, all the chariots of Egypt, officers on every one — is erased. The army that chased them into the sea does not emerge from the sea.

**v.30 — "ISRAEL SAW EGYPT DEAD ON THE SEASHORE" (Heb. *vayyosha YHWH bayyom hahu et-Yisra'el miyyad Mitsrayim vayyar Yisra'el et-Mitsrayim met al-sefat hayyam*, וַיּ֨וֹשַׁע יְהוָ֜ה בַּיּ֥וֹם הַה֛וּא אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל מִיַּ֣ד מִצְרָ֑יִם וַיַּ֤רְא יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ אֶת־מִצְרַ֔יִם מֵ֖ת עַל־שְׂפַ֥ת הַיָּֽם):** The verb *vayyosha* (ויושע, from *yasha'*, ישע, "to save, to deliver") — the same root as *yeshu'ah* in v.13 — is now fulfilled: what Moses promised, YHWH has accomplished. The phrase *miyyad Mitsrayim* (מיד מצרים, "from the hand of Egypt") uses *yad* ("hand") — the Egyptians' hand has been broken; YHWH's hand (*beyad chazaqah*) has prevailed.

The visual image is stark: *vayyar Yisra'el et-Mitsrayim met al-sefat hayyam* — "Israel saw Egypt dead on the shore of the sea." The singular *met* (מת, "dead") used collectively is more devastating than a plural would be: Egypt is dead. Not "the Egyptians were dead" but "Egypt, dead." The entire civilization's military power, reduced to a singular adjective: dead. The *sefat hayyam* (שפת הים, "lip/shore of the sea") uses *safah* (שפה, "lip"), the same word for human lips — the sea's edge is its lip, and the bodies wash up onto it. The sea speaks death with its lip.

**v.31 — FAITH BORN FROM SEEING (Heb. *vayyar Yisra'el et-hayyad haggedolah asher asah YHWH beMitsrayim vayyir'u ha'am et-YHWH vayya'aminu baYHWH uveMosheh avdo*, וַיַּ֨רְא יִשְׂרָאֵ֜ל אֶת־הַיָּ֣ד הַגְּדֹלָ֗ה אֲשֶׁ֨ר עָשָׂ֤ה יְהוָה֙ בְּמִצְרַ֔יִם וַיִּֽירְא֥וּ הָעָ֖ם אֶת־יְהוָ֑ה וַיַּֽאֲמִ֨ינוּ֙ בַּיהוָ֔ה וּבְמֹשֶׁ֖ה עַבְדּֽוֹ):** This verse is the theological climax of the entire exodus narrative. Three verbs in sequence: *vayyar* (ויר, "they saw"), *vayyir'u* (ויראו, "they feared/revered"), *vayya'aminu* (ויאמינו, "they believed/trusted"). Sight leads to awe, and awe leads to faith. The progression is not from faith to sight (as in later Christian theology) but from sight to faith: Israel sees the great hand, experiences reverential fear, and then trusts. This is the Hebrew Bible's foundational model of *emunah* (אמונה, "faithfulness, trust, belief"): faith is not blind — it is the response of people who have seen what God does.

The *yad haggedolah* (יד הגדלה, "the great hand") returns the motif that has run through chapters 3-14: YHWH's hand versus Pharaoh's hand. The contest is decided. The hand that created the plagues, parted the sea, and destroyed the army is *gedolah* — great, powerful, overwhelming. Pharaoh's hand is at the bottom of the sea.

The verb *vayya'aminu* (ויאמינו, from *aman*, אמן, "to be firm, to be reliable, to trust") — the root of *amen*, *emunah*, *emet* (truth) — appears here in the Hiphil: "they caused themselves to trust," "they put their trust in." The construction *ba-YHWH* (ביהוה, "in YHWH") indicates trust directed toward a person, not assent to a proposition. They did not believe *that* YHWH exists; they believed *in* YHWH — they entrusted themselves to him. And also *uveMosheh avdo* (ובמשה עבדו, "and in Moses his servant") — faith in God extends to faith in God's chosen agent. Moses is not divine, but he is authenticated by the event. The title *avdo* (עבדו, "his servant") places Moses in the same *eved/avod* framework as the entire exodus: Israel was *avadim* (servants/slaves) to Pharaoh; now Moses is *eved* (servant) to YHWH. The word has been redeemed. Service is no longer slavery — it is the vocation of the one who stands between God and the people.

---

**The theology of Exodus 14 — the sea divided, the enemy drowned, faith born:**

This chapter is the climax toward which the entire Exodus narrative has been building since chapter 1. Everything converges here: the hardening, the plagues, the Passover, the departure, the pillar, the pursuit, and now the sea. Four theological movements complete the arc:

**1. The divine trap.** Vv.1-4 reveal that God is not merely reacting to Pharaoh's pursuit — he is engineering it. The geography of entrapment (sea, wilderness, Egyptian outpost) is deliberately arranged to lure Pharaoh into his final act of aggression. This raises the most difficult theological question of the narrative: God hardens Pharaoh's heart *in order to destroy him*. The hardening is not merely permissive; it is purposive. God stiffens the tyrant so that the tyrant will charge into the sea. The text does not flinch from this. YHWH's glory (*kavod*) is displayed through the total defeat of the one whose heart was made heavy (*kaved*).

**2. The complaint and the command.** Vv.10-14 introduce what will become Israel's defining flaw: the complaint that prefers the security of slavery to the danger of freedom. "Were there no graves in Egypt?" is the rhetorical question that will echo through forty years of wilderness wandering. And Moses' response — "Stand firm, see, be silent" — establishes the counter-principle: trust is the antidote to complaint, and trust is possible because YHWH fights.

**3. The passage through death.** Vv.21-22 describe what is simultaneously a military deliverance, a cosmological event, and a liturgical prototype. The east wind that blows all night recalls the *ruach Elohim* of Genesis 1:2 hovering over the waters. The splitting of the sea echoes the separation of waters in Genesis 1:6-7. The dry ground emerging from the water is creation happening again — land appears where there was only water, just as in Genesis 1:9. Israel walks through the sea as the new creation walks through the new world. The walls of water on right and left form a birth canal — Israel is being born as a nation, passing through water into a new existence. This passage will become the archetype for every subsequent deliverance in Scripture: the waters of Noah, the crossing of the Jordan, the baptismal waters of the New Testament.

**4. Faith from sight.** V.31 establishes that Israel's faith is empirical before it is theological. They see, then they fear, then they trust. This is not credulous belief; it is the rational response to overwhelming evidence. The God who said "I will harden," "I will be glorified," "they will know that I am YHWH" has now done every single thing he said he would do. Faith, in this moment, is simply the acknowledgment that this God keeps his word. The *emunah* born at the seashore is the foundation on which Sinai will be built — but it is also fragile, as the golden calf and the wilderness complaints will soon demonstrate. Israel believes here, at the peak of the miracle; whether they can sustain that belief in the ordinary days ahead is the question that drives the rest of the Pentateuch.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 14:**

| Verse | Hebrew | Stem | Subject | Translation |
|-------|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| v.4 | *va'achazzeq* (וחזקתי) | Piel (intensive) | YHWH | "I will harden/strengthen" |
| v.8 | *vayechazzeq* (ויחזק) | Piel (intensive) | YHWH | "YHWH hardened/strengthened" |
| v.17 | *va'ani hineni mechazzeq* (ואני הנני מחזק) | Piel participle (intensive) | YHWH | "I am about to harden/strengthen" |

All three occurrences in chapter 14 use *chazaq* (חזק) in the Piel stem with YHWH as the explicit subject. This is the final phase of the hardening cycle. In the plague narrative, the hardening alternated between Pharaoh hardening his own heart (*kaved/qashah*, self-caused) and YHWH hardening it (*chazaq* Piel, divinely caused). By chapter 14, the alternation has ceased. YHWH alone hardens. Pharaoh's agency, which was present in the early plagues, has been absorbed into the divine purpose. The tyrant who began by choosing stubbornness now has stubbornness imposed upon him. Whether this represents the loss of free will or the culmination of choices already freely made is the theological question the text deliberately leaves open.

**Cumulative hardening count through Exodus 14:** The verb *chazaq* Piel (YHWH-caused) now accounts for the final three hardening references in the narrative. Combined with the earlier occurrences — *kaved* (Pharaoh's self-hardening in 8:11, 8:28, 9:7, 9:34), *chazaq* Piel (YHWH-caused in 9:12, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10), *qashah* (Pharaoh in 13:15 retelling) — the total pattern shows a gradual divine takeover of the hardening process, reaching its completion here at the sea.

— *PNEUMA*
